title: Reflexive pronouns
description: Learn when and when not to use reflexive pronouns.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Jamal cut  \_\_\_\_\_\_ shaving.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, Jamal is both the one doing the cutting and the one being cut, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"himself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Heidi looked at  \_\_\_\_\_\_ reflection in the pond.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, Heidi is the subject, but not the object (which is "reflection").

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the possessive adjective **"her"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Milosh didn't care if Evana was mean to  \_\_\_\_\_\_; he loved her.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "Evana" is the one being mean, but she's being mean to "Milosh", not herself. 

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"him"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Zofia caught  \_\_\_\_\_\_ on the doorknob leaving the house.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, Jamal is both the one doing the catching and the one being caught, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"herself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>The ducks preened  \_\_\_\_\_\_ in the sunshine.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, the ducks are both the ones doing the preening and the ones being preened, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"themselves"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>As members of the marching band paraded noisily through the library, nobody could ignore  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "nobody" is the one doing the ignoring, but "members of the marching band" are the ones being ignored.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"them"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>One must take care to protect  \_\_\_\_\_\_ when traveling alone.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, "one" is both the noun doing the protecting and the noun being protected, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"oneself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Will you listen to  \_\_\_\_\_\_?  You're being absurd.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, "you" is both the one doing the listening and the one being listened too, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"yourself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>The snail dragged  \_\_\_\_\_\_ onto the leaf.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, the snail is both the one doing the dragging and the one being dragged, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"itself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Unable to wake  \_\_\_\_\_\_ with yelling, Mom resorted to a pair of cymbals.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "mom" is the one doing the waking, but "we" are the ones being woken up.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"us"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Abigail was nervous about her presentation, but Isabella told  \_\_\_\_\_\_ it would be okay.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "Isabella" is the one doing the telling, but "Abigail" is the one being told.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"her"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Audrey built a sand castle at the beach and then knocked  \_\_\_\_\_\_ down.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "Audrey" is the one doing the knocking, but "the sand castle" is the thing being knocked down.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"it"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>What do you see  \_\_\_\_\_\_ doing in five years?

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, "you" are both the one doing the seeing and the one being seen, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"yourself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>If you have any questions, please email  \_\_\_\_\_\_ or Addison.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "you" are the one doing the emailing, but "I" am the one being emailed.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"me"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Debbie said goodbye to Sam and waved at  \_\_\_\_\_\_ as the train pulled out of the station.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "Debbie" is the one doing the waving, but "Sam" is the one being waved at.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"him"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>All alone, Jessie hummed quietly to  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, Jessie is both the one doing the humming and the one being hummed to, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"herself"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>A fly appeared in the frog’s field of vision. The frog captured  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "the frog" is the one doing the capturing, but the "fly" is the one being captured.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"it"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Mom caught  \_\_\_\_\_\_ sneaking a cookie before dinner.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._

In this sentence, "Mom" is the one doing the catching, but "I" am the one being caught.

So, instead of a reflexive pronoun, we should use the pronoun **"me"**.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Choose the best pronoun for this sentence.**

>Ginny almost yelled at the boy running across the traffic-clogged street, but stopped  \_\_\_\_\_\_.

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We use reflexive pronouns when the *subject* and the *object* of a sentence are the same thing.

Examples of reflexive pronouns: _myself, yourself, himself, herself, ourselves, themselves._
In this sentence, Ginny is both the one doing the stopping and the one being stopped, so we should use the reflexive pronoun **"herself"**.
